questions.explanation: NavigationAgent2D calculates paths around obstacles using a NavigationRegion2D as the walkable area. It gives you the next_path_position() to move toward each frame.

lessons.title: Structuring a Full Game Scene
lessons.content:
A well-structured game scene is easy to navigate and maintain. Here is a pattern that works for most 2D games.

The Level scene root is a Node2D. Its children:

World — a Node2D containing all physical game objects: the tilemap, static bodies, spawners, and level geometry. Everything that exists in 2D world space goes here.

Entities — a Node2D containing dynamic objects: the player, enemies, pickups. Keeping these separate from world geometry makes iteration easier.

HUD — a CanvasLayer containing all UI: health bar, score label, pause menu. Always a CanvasLayer so it stays fixed on screen.

GameManager — a local Node (or reference to the Autoload) that coordinates scene-level logic: level completion, spawning waves, handling events between entities.

This is not a rigid rule — it is a starting point. As your project grows you will develop your own conventions. The key principle: group things by their relationship to the world (world space vs screen space) and by how frequently they change (static geometry vs dynamic entities).

questions.q: Why separate World geometry from Entities in a level scene?
options: It makes the scene tree clearer to navigate and allows independent iteration on geometry and gameplay.
World nodes cannot be siblings of Entity nodes.
It is required by Godot's physics engine.
questions.explanation: Organization is about human readability. A clear scene tree hierarchy means you always know where to look for something and can change one group without accidentally touching another.

questions.q: Why should HUD always be inside a CanvasLayer in a level scene?
options: CanvasLayer keeps UI at fixed screen positions independent of the camera and world transforms.
Godot requires CanvasLayer for any Control node in a 2D scene.
CanvasLayer provides automatic z-ordering for UI.
questions.explanation: Without CanvasLayer, Control nodes scroll with the camera. CanvasLayer pins them to the screen at all times — essential for HUD elements.

lessons.title: Diagnostic: Assemble a Complete Level
lessons.prompt: Build a complete Level scene structure. Root should be a Node2D named 'Level'. Add a Node2D named 'World' and a Node2D named 'Entities' as children of Level. Add a CanvasLayer named 'HUD' as a child of Level. Add a Node named 'LevelManager' as a child of Level. The script should reference the LevelManager and connect a custom signal 'level_complete' to a function called _on_level_complete.

validation.message: Add @onready var level_manager = $LevelManager.
Connect level_manager.level_complete to _on_level_complete inside _ready().

lessons.title: ShaderMaterial — Visual Effects
lessons.content:
Shaders are programs that run on the GPU and control exactly how pixels are rendered. Godot uses a shader language called Godot Shading Language (GLSL-like). You apply a ShaderMaterial to any CanvasItem (Sprite2D, TextureRect, etc.) to override how it draws.

A canvas_item shader has a fragment() function. It runs per-pixel and sets COLOR — the final RGBA output. TEXTURE is the node's original texture. TEXTURE_PIXEL_SIZE gives the size of one pixel in UV space.

Common effects: outline (sample neighboring pixels, color any transparent neighbor), dissolve (discard pixels based on a noise texture and a threshold), color replacement (swap one color for another), wave distortion (offset UV coordinates over time).

You pass data from GDScript into a shader using set_shader_parameter(): material.set_shader_parameter("dissolve_amount", 0.5). Animate the parameter over time with a Tween for transitions.

For simple color effects, ColorRect with a ShaderMaterial is often faster and more flexible than trying to achieve the same result with nested Control nodes.

examples.label: Simple outline shader and GDScript control

questions.q: Where does the fragment() function in a canvas_item shader run?
questions.explanation: The fragment() function runs on the GPU once for every pixel of the rendered output. This is what allows per-pixel effects like outlines, dissolves, and distortion.

questions.q: How do you pass a value from GDScript into a shader at runtime?
options: You cannot pass values at runtime.
questions.explanation: set_shader_parameter() on a ShaderMaterial sends a value to a uniform variable declared in the shader. Animate it with Tween for smooth transitions.

lessons.title: Exporting Your Game
lessons.content:
When your game is ready to share, you export it to a standalone file that runs without the Godot editor.

First install export templates: Editor > Manage Export Templates > Download. Templates are version-specific — download the ones matching your Godot version.

Then configure an export preset: Project > Export > Add a preset for your target platform (Windows, macOS, Linux, HTML5, Android, iOS). Set the executable name, icon, and any platform-specific options.

For HTML5/Web export the game runs in a browser. This is the easiest way to let others play without installing anything — upload the exported folder to itch.io or GitHub Pages. Note that some features behave differently on Web: threading requires specific server headers, and file access uses a virtual filesystem.

For desktop exports, Godot produces a .exe (Windows), a .app (macOS), or a binary (Linux) plus a .pck file containing your game data. You can embed the .pck into the executable for a single-file distribution.

res:// paths remain valid in exports but the underlying files are packed — you cannot browse to them. user:// is always writable and is where save files should go.

Test your export early. Bugs that only appear in exported builds — missing resources, platform-specific input differences, file path case sensitivity on Linux — are much easier to catch before you have built the whole game.

questions.q: Why must you install export templates before exporting?
options: They are the pre-compiled runtime for each platform that Godot packages your game into.
They configure the game's display settings.
They are required only for mobile platforms.
questions.explanation: Export templates are the platform-specific Godot runtimes. Your game data is packed alongside them. Without the template, Godot has nothing to package your game into.

questions.q: Where should save files be written in an exported game?
questions.explanation: res:// is read-only in exported games — it is packed into the game archive. user:// always points to a writable application data folder on the user's machine.

questions.q: What is the easiest way to let someone play your Godot game without installing anything?
options: Export for HTML5 and host it on itch.io or GitHub Pages.
Export for Windows and send the .exe.
questions.explanation: HTML5 export produces a folder you upload to any web host. Players open a URL in their browser — no download, no install.

lessons.title: Performance Basics
lessons.content:
Godot is fast enough for most 2D games without any optimization. But knowing a few basic principles saves you from common mistakes.

Do not use get_node() or $ in _process or _physics_process. Cache node references in @onready variables at _ready() time. Resolving paths every frame is unnecessary and slow.

Avoid creating new objects in _process. Instantiating scenes and creating Arrays or Dictionaries every frame allocates memory that the garbage collector then has to clean up. Create things once and reuse them.

Object pooling: instead of queue_free()-ing a bullet and instantiating a new one for every shot, maintain a pool of pre-created bullets. Grab one from the pool, use it, return it to the pool. This eliminates allocation overhead for frequently spawned objects.

Use call_deferred() when you need to change something but cannot do it mid-physics-process — such as removing a node or changing a scene. It defers the call to the end of the frame.

Profile before optimizing. Godot's built-in Profiler (Debugger > Profiler) shows exactly which functions are taking the most time. Optimize what the profiler says is slow, not what you think is slow.

questions.q: Why should you cache node references in @onready instead of calling $NodeName every frame?
options: Resolving node paths every frame is slower than storing a reference once.
$ cannot be used inside _process in Godot 4.
questions.explanation: Path resolution traverses the scene tree every time. @onready resolves once at startup and stores a direct reference. Inside a loop that runs 60 times per second, this matters.

questions.q: What is object pooling?
options: A way to share textures between multiple sprites.
Reusing pre-created objects instead of instantiating and freeing them repeatedly.
A method for streaming large levels.
questions.explanation: Pooling eliminates repeated instantiation overhead. Keep a set of dormant objects, activate one when needed, deactivate it when done — much faster than create/destroy cycles.

questions.q: When should you use `call_deferred()` instead of calling a function directly?
options: Whenever you want a function to run faster.
When you need to modify the scene tree from within _physics_process or a signal handler.
Only for functions that take longer than one frame.
questions.explanation: Modifying the scene tree mid-physics (removing nodes, changing scenes) can crash Godot. call_deferred() queues the call for safe execution at the end of the frame.
